title: What F1 Pit Stop Strategy Taught Me About ML Model Selection

body.text: Every race weekend, engineers at Mercedes or Red Bull face the same core problem as any ML practitioner: too many variables, not enough certainty, and a decision that needs to be made right now.
In F1, the undercut is when you pit your driver one lap earlier than your rival — banking on fresh tyres and clear air to build enough gap before they pit. It works brilliantly. Until it doesn't.
You don't pick the perfect model. You pick the best one given the data you have and the time you have left.

When I was building the phishing detector, I had 47 features extracted from URLs. I didn't use all of them. Like a strategist who ignores tyre temperature data on a cold track, I dropped features that added noise rather than signal.

items: Presence of HTTPS — surprisingly low signal in modern phishing

body.text: The Code That Made the Call

from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif

selector = SelectKBest(score_func=f_classif, k=12)
X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)

# Like a pit wall engineer — keep only what moves the needle
SelectKBest is my pit wall. It scores each feature and keeps only the top k. Brutal. Efficient. Exactly right.
The best strategists and the best ML engineers share one trait: they commit to a decision under uncertainty and adjust fast when the data changes. That's the whole game.
